coredata.dc:title: Interface dipole evolution from the hybrid coupling between nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots and polyethylenimine featuring the electron transport thin layer at Al/Si interfaces

Chen, Chia-Yun
coredata.dc:description: The assessment of electron transport layer (ETL) for rear-contact engineering of silicon (Si) based optoelectronics has been considered as one of the critical challenges that leverage the performance improvement and device reliability. In this work, the hybrid design of ETL, obtained from the solution-processed nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) incorporated with organic polyethylenimine (PEI) demonstrates the feasible contact characteristics for the modification of Si/Al contacts, which greatly facilitates the transport and collection of photoexcited electrons in the Si-based optoelectronics. The aspects of microstructures, functional groups, chemical features, interfacial characteristics and band structures of NCQD/PEI are explicated, visualizing that the evolution of interface dipoles mediated by the overall outcome of physisorption and chemisorption effects, modifies the surface potential difference and results in the explicit reduction of the Al work function from 4.3 eV for pristine Al to 3.23 eV based on the optimized constitutional design (0.10 % NCQD in PEI). These findings are practically employed on the Si-based hybrid solar cells at Si/Al interfaces, fulfilling the conversion-efficiency improvement by 30.9 % compared with reference cells without ETL employment, which are experimentally interpreted by the efficient electron transport across the Si/Al heterojunction and charge collection.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The assessment of electron transport layer (ETL) for rear-contact engineering of silicon (Si) based optoelectronics has been considered as one of the critical challenges that leverage the performance improvement and device reliability. In this work, the hybrid design of ETL, obtained from the solution-processed nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) incorporated with organic polyethylenimine (PEI) demonstrates the feasible contact characteristics for the modification of Si/Al contacts, which greatly facilitates the transport and collection of photoexcited electrons in the Si-based optoelectronics. The aspects of microstructures, functional groups, chemical features, interfacial characteristics and band structures of NCQD/PEI are explicated, visualizing that the evolution of interface dipoles mediated by the overall outcome of physisorption and chemisorption effects, modifies the surface potential difference and results in the explicit reduction of the Al work function from 4.3 eV for pristine Al to 3.23 eV based on the optimized constitutional design (0.10 % NCQD in PEI). These findings are practically employed on the Si-based hybrid solar cells at Si/Al interfaces, fulfilling the conversion-efficiency improvement by 30.9 % compared with reference cells without ETL employment, which are experimentally interpreted by the efficient electron transport across the Si/Al heterojunction and charge collection.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The energy crisis has been considered an emerging issue worldwide owing to the increasing growth of power consumption as well as the depletion of effective resources. Sustainable sources of energy are particularly essential as they potentially suppress the reliance on carbon-based fossil fuels. Silicon (Si), an abundant semiconductor material with mature fabrication technologies and a complete chain from industrial manufacturing to practical employment, has been considered one of the mainstreams for various renewable power sources for electricity supply, such as solar cells [1–3], photocatalytic employment [4,5], Li-ion battery [6] and thermoelectric materials [7]. One critically technical aspect of Si-based devices correlates with the unavoidable formation of a Schottky contact between Si and the employed electrode. A Schottky junction is normally created owing to the fact that the metal work function is larger than the work function of Si with semiconductor nature, resulting in charge transfer from the semiconductor to the metal until the Fermi energy levels align at equilibrium [8]. When a Schottky junction is created, the semiconductor band edge bends upward toward the direction of the electric field aligning from the positively polarized site (Si) to the negatively polarized site (Al metal). These circumstances hinder the electron collection at the Al electrode and electron-hole pairs are easily trapped and recombined as the Schottky barrier is created in the vicinity of the junction sites, leading to limited charge extraction and collection at the Si/Al contact [9], where these issues are also demanded for exploiting the industry-compatible surface treatment on Al [10] and TiN/Ti [11] substrates.

Enormous efforts have been made to overcome the bottleneck of Schottky contact. Elaboration of electron-transport layer (ETL) at the Si/Al contact has been considered one of the promising and reliable solutions for the manufacturing of power-efficient Si-based integrated circuits (ICs) with near noise immunity [12]. To meet the requirements of current IC designs where each electronic element has to be miniaturized, the critical minimum dimensionality of electronic devices has been reduced to nanoscale, which not only shrinks the device sizes efficiently, but also provides excellent operational performance. In this aspect, interface engineering in the ETL design to provide efficient carrier selectivity is one of the promising strategies [13,14]. Although the employment of reliable n-type doping at the rear interface, termed as a back surface field (BSF), displays the reliable strategy toward the employment of ETL as an efficient carrier-selective contact and has been extensively adopted in industrial manufacturing of Si-based solar cells, the requirement of high processing temperature (800 °C–1000 °C) turns to be undesirable for the practicality of polymer-involved devices and applications [15]. In this context, organic ETLs have become the alternative materials for ETL deposition. Polyethylenimine (PEI), as the Liquid cationic polymer with abundant amino functional groups, has been extensively reported as the efficient electron selective layer, illustrating significant improvements in the performance of Si-bases optoelectronics, originating from the chemical nature of Lewis basicity that results in downward band bending near the Si side due to the positive polarity of metal surfaces modified with PEI coating [16]. On the other hand, the colloid composite layer comprising cesium carbonate (Cs2CO3) mixed polyethylenimine (PEI) [17] and poly[(9,9-bis(3′-(N,N-diethylamino)propyl)-2,7-fluorene)-alt-2,7-(9,9-dioctylfluorene)] (PFN) can further improve the interfacial conductance by reducing the contact resistance between rear interfaces as well as effective electron collection based on the formed Ohmic contact [18]. Aside from that, heterojunction-engineered designs, such as Si/MgO [19] and Ni-Si/MgO [20] have been considered the promising strategies for tailoring the optoelectronic and electrical properties of Si, although they are mainly exploited for improving the catalytic characteristics of Si [21,22].

To further assess the ETL features based on a low-temperature solution process and at the same time, offer reliable manufacturing capability compatible with the integration of Si-based microelectronics, the replacement of non-sustainable fillers in the formation of PEI-based ETLs with the green-synthesized quantum dots is necessary. Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) emerge as highly potential low-dimensional semiconductor materials possessing several advantages including adjustable band configuration, photostability and green manufacturing capability [23–26], which can be considered for tailoring the reduction of contact resistance and performance improvement of the Si/Al contact in Si-based devices. More importantly, the NCQDs’ outer shells are surrounded by polar functional groups such as carboxyl and carbonyl groups, which facilitate the good dispersion of NCQDs as fillers within the PEI matrix retaining the aqueous nature and can be directly deposited onto Si surfaces with IC-compatible and conventional spin-coating method. Therefore, in this work, the design of hybrid ETL structures comprising various amounts of NCQDs within PEI is examined. The explicit capability for the modulation of the Al work function and in turn features the efficient ETL design for the Si/Al contact is visualized.

α-D(+)-glucose (Mw = 180.16 g mol−1, 99 % purity), L-cysteine (Mw = 121.16 g mol−1, 98+ % purity), Ethylene Glycol (Mw = 62.07 g mol−1, 99+ % purity) and silver nitrate (Mw = 169.87 g mol−1, 99.85 % purity) were obtained from Acros Organics. Branched-poly(ethlenimine) (Mw ∼750,000, 50 %wt in H2O) and hydrogen peroxide solution (Mw = 34.01 g mol−1, 34.5-36.5 % assay) were purchased from Sigma Aldrich. Poly(3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene) polystyrene sulfonate (PH1000) was from Heraeus CleviouTM Uni-Region Biotech. Zonyl FS300 fluorinated surfactant was from Abcr GmbH &CO.KG. Ammonium hydroxide solution (Mw = 35.04 g mol−1, 30-33 % in H2O), hydrofluoric acid (Mw = 20.01 g mol−1, 49 % assay) and nitric acid (Mw = 63.01 g mol−1, ≥ 65 %) were purchased from Honeywell. The utilized water in the experiments was purified through a Millipore water purification system (18 MΩ, Milli-Q, Millipore).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of N-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis of NCQDs was started by mixing 0.10 g of α-D-glucose and 0.20 g of L-cysteine in 10.00 mL of DI water under ultrasonic sonication. The transparent solution was heated in a commercial microwave (Panasonic NN-ST25JW) at 700 W for 3 min, and the obtained yellow-brown precipitates were dissolved in 10.00 mL of DI water and followed by sonication treatment for 10 min. The mixture was filtered through a 0.20 µm syringe filter to carefully remove the residual particles and was dialyzed against DI water in a dialysis membrane (1 kDa) for 6 h. The as-prepared CQD suspensions were dried in the oven at 80 ºC to obtain the solid CQDs for further use. The schematic illustrations for the synthetic procedures are presented in Fig. S1 of the Supplementary material.

The preparation of the PEI precursor was performed by mixing 10 ml of DI water with 0.1 g of PEI reagent, which is termed the sole PEI-based ETL (0.01 %w/v PEI). In addition, the preparation of three different NCQD-PEI ETLs was performed by mixing the 0.01 %w/v PEI with various amounts of NCQD: 0.005 g, 0.01 g and 0.015 g under the magnetic stirring 75 ºC for 4 hr, termed as the 0.05 %, 0.10 % and 0.15 % w/v of NCQDs-PEI, respectively. Subsequently, the film deposition was readily conducted on Si substrates via a standard spin-coating method at 4000 rpm for 60 s, and no further annealing process was required. The thickness of the deposited ETLs on Si substrates was directly patterned using HCl (15 %) to remove parts of the ETL structures, and then the ETL thickness was determined by AFM scan.

The standard single-crystalline n-type Si substrates (100) with the size of 2 × 2 cm2 and 0.1-0.5 Ω.cm resistivity were utilized, with the substrates being rinsed with DI water, acetone and isopropyl alcohol (IPA) sequentially for several cycles. After that, the oriented Si nanowire arrays (SiNWs) with a controlled length of 400±5.3 nm were directly formed via Ag-assisted chemical etching by immersing the cleaned Si substrates in 4.6 M hydrofluoric acid (HF) and 0.01 M silver nitrate (AgNO3) at room temperature for 3.5 min [27–30]. The residual Ag particles were then dissolved by dipping the samples in 65 % nitric acid and rinsing with DI water. Subsequently, the removal of native oxide grown on SiNW surfaces was carried out by dipping the SiNW/Si samples in diluted HF (HF: H2O = 1:9) for 30 s [31], and they were eventually dried with gentle N2 gas.

Spin coating of the NCQD-PEI solutions at the rear side of SiNW/Si substrates was performed at a spin rate of 4000 rpm for 60 s. Subsequently, the Al electrode with a thickness of 150 nm was directly deposited using the electron-beam deposition. After that, double-layered PEDOT:PSS films were prepared at the front side of the SiNW/Si cells using the spin-coating method: The first layer of PEDOT:PSS was prepared with two sets of spin cycles for attaining sound surface coverage on SiNAs (1000 rpm for 10 s and 8000 rpm for 50 s), followed by an annealing process at 140 ºC for 2 min. Then, the second-layered PEDOT:PSS film serving as conductive hole transport layer (HTL) was successively prepared with a spin rate of 1000 rpm for 60 s, and then annealed at 140 ºC for 15 min. Finally, Ag grids with the thickness of 300 nm as the top electrode were deposited with the assistance of shallow mask using the electron-beam deposition. In these measurements, 10 samples were tested for each condition, and the mean values and error bars are presented.

Light-absorption characteristics of samples were measured with a UV/visible spectrophotometer (Hitachi U3900H) covering the spectral range of 200-800 nm. Static photoluminescence (PL) spectra (Horiba Fluoromax-4) of the samples were examined under different excitation wavelengths of 280-520 nm, respectively. The microstructures of the samples were investigated with High-Resolution Transmission Electron Microscopy (HR-TEM, JEM-2100F). Crystallographic characterizations were carried out with the X-ray diffractometer (XRD, Bruker AXS Gmbh). Spectral analyses of chemical compositions and surface chemical configurations were performed by Fourier Transform Infrared Spectrometer (FTIR, Themo Scientific Nicolet 6700) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, PHI 5000 Versa Probe), respectively. Surface polarity and QD dimensions in aqueous solutions were examined with the measurements of zeta potential using the Dynamic Light Scattering instrument (DLS, Malvern Zetasizer Nano S). The sheet resistance and thickness of the prepared ETLs were estimated using four-point probe resistance instrument (Keithley 2400) and a Spectroscopic ellipsometer (J.A. Woolam M2000-DI), respectively. Surface topography of ETLs and potential difference between Al/ETL/n-Si interfaces were conducted with multi-functional scanning probe microscope (Bruker). Molecular orbital and work function of materials and metals were performed by Ultraviolet photoelectron spectroscopy (UPS, ULVAC-PHI PHI 5000 Versaprobe II). The photovoltaic performances were characterized under an illuminated solar simulator at AM 1.5 G with a calibrated intensity of 100 mW cm−2, and the excited photocurrents were monitored using a current–voltage measurement system (Keithley 2400).

Optical characteristics of the synthesized NCQDs were examined with light-absorption and photoemission features are shown in Figs. 1(a) and 1(b), respectively. The broadband absorption phenomena covering spectral regions from 200 nm to 450 nm are displayed with the shoulder band below 250 nm and a broad peak at the band centered at 330-350 nm being characterized. These two main features are assigned to the π-π* transition of sp2 hybridized carbon in NCQD cores and the n-π* transition of hydrophilic passivation groups, respectively [32]. In addition, the pronounced PL emissions can be observed under the excitation wavelengths (λex) ranging from 280 to 520 nm, displaying the most intense PL peak centered at 440 nm (λex = 360 nm), as depicted in Fig. 1(b). By deconvoluting the correlated PL peak in the spectral domain, two emission behaviors arising from distinct photophysical origins were identified: Emission feature observed from lower excitation wavelengths (λex= 280-320 nm) displays the λex-independent light-emission characteristics, which can be attributed to the appearance of structural defects, normally existing in the form of lattice vacancies or irregular carbon rings [32]. Meanwhile, the relatively longer excitation wavelengths (λex= 360 nm above) display the λex-dependent PL behavior, which can be ascribed to the radiative recombination of electron-hole pairs confined within the zero-dimensional feature of NCQDs [33]. These two distinct PL features determined by the excitation wavelength of LED lights can be clearly visualized by normalizing the correlated PL intensities, as evidenced in the inset of Fig. 1(b).

In addition, the morphological and crystallographic configurations of the NCQDs were characterized. The highly uniform and monodispersed NCQDs with sizes in the range of 4.7±1.5 nm were statistically estimated from TEM observations [Fig. 1(c)], and the correlated lattice fringes corresponding to the lattice spacing of 0.24 nm (1120) of crystallite graphitic planes were indexed in HRTEM characterizations, as demonstrated in Fig. 1(d). The XRD pattern of NCQDs is illustrated in Fig. 2(a). The indexed spectrum displays the characteristic diffraction peak at 18.89 degrees, which corresponds to an interlayer spacing of 0.47 nm for (002) graphite oxide [34]. Several minor peaks at 26.50 and 32.60 are also observed, corresponding to (002) and (020) planes of graphite carbon, respectively.

Spectral examinations of the functional groups of the as-prepared NCQDs were performed by FTIR characterizations, as illustrated in Fig. 2(b). In addition, spectral analyses on L-cysteine and glucose were conducted to clarify the existence of residual precursors in the obtained NCQD samples. The results feature the spectral patterns of sp2 carbon bond (C=C) at 1635.4 cm−1, in conjunction with two broad peaks at 3426.9 and 3249.5 cm−1 assigned to O-H stretching and N-H stretching vibrations, respectively. In addition, relatively weak absorption bands at 1764.6, 1349.9 and 1207.2 cm−1 were observed, corresponding to the vibrations of C=O stretching, N-H bending and O-H bending, respectively, indicating the presence of carboxyl, amino and hydroxyl groups on the surface of sp2 carbon, respectively [35]. Furthermore, Fig. 2(c) presents the survey XPS analysis, which quantitatively displays the constitutional amount at sample surfaces, including carbon (83.2 at %), oxygen (12.1 at %) and nitrogen (4.6 at %), obtained from the integrated areas at the corresponding spectral features of 285.4, 531.5 and 399.9 eV, respectively, indicating the successful formation of NCQDs. High-resolution XPS spectra of C1s is displayed in Fig. 2(d), where the distinct peaks at 284.7, 286.5, 288.5 and 285.5 eV are attributed to C=C, C-O, C=O and C-N bonds, respectively [36]. On the other hand, the XPS signatures of C=O and C-OH bonds were displayed at the binding energies of 531.6 and 533 eV, respectively, from examining the high-resolution scan of the O1s spectrum [Fig. 2(e)] [37]. The XPS peaks at 398.2 eV, 399.4 eV and 400.3 eV were observed from the high-resolution N1s peaks, assigned to chemical bonds of pyridinic nitrogen, 1˚/2˚ amine and pyrrolic nitrogen, respectively, as shown in Fig. 2(f) [38].

Optical and electric characterizations of the PEI layer incorporated with NCQDs were further investigated under the considerations of three various NCQD concentrations, including 0.05 %, 0.10 % and 0.15 %, respectively. Fig. 3(a) illustrates that all three NCQDs/PEI layers present similar light-absorption behaviors covering the spectral range of 200 nm to 450 nm, whereas the characteristic absorption features become more pronounced with an increased amount of NCQD fillers within NCQD-PEI layers. In addition, PL characterizations of NCQD-PEI composite layers were conducted to investigate the emission behaviors after incorporating various concentrations of NCQDs into the PEI matrix, as plotted in Fig. 3(b). The explicit λex-independent PL emission when excited at 280-320 nm and λex-dependent PL behavior at longer excitation wavelengths are validated that display the consistent luminescent phenomena with barely dispersed NCQDs, as shown in Fig. 1(b). It is also noted to show that the PL spectra of NCQD-PEI and NCQDs are almost identical under the excitation wavelength of 360 nm by visualizing the similar PL peak at 440 nm, which suggests the absence of PL quenching after incorporating PEI molecules with NCQDs, as evidenced in the inset figure of Fig. 3(b). In addition, the layer thickness of the three prepared NCQD-PEI films deposited on Si substrates was measured using topographic AFM analysis, where the estimated thicknesses slightly increase with the increase of NCQD addition, showing 2.18 nm (0, pristine PEI), 2.50 nm (0.05 %), 2.54 nm (0.10 %) to 2.56 nm (0.15 %), as measured by AFM analysis on the patterned ETL structures, as discussed in the Experimental section. The microstructures of the NCQD-PEI hybrid structures are examined with HRTEM characterizations, as presented in Fig. S2 of the Supplementary material. The observed NCQDs exhibit quasispherical features with crystallographic lattice spacings of 0.24 nm for the (1120) plane, which is in agreement with the pristine NCQDs except that the surfaces of NCQDs are surrounded by shell-like PEI chains.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characteristic curves of Ohmic contact between n-Si/Al interface after NCQD-PEI layer insertion
ce:section.ce:para: To further study the Al/n-type Si contact after the insertion of NCQD-PEI layer at the interfaces of 150-nm thick Al and Si substrates, the current-voltage (I-V) characteristic curves were measured, as shown in Fig. 4(a) The electric characteristic of typical Al/n-Si without NCQD-PEI coating displays the Schottky-contact phenomenon and the involvement of Schottky barrier height between the metal and n-Si restricts the electron transport to the Al cathode. By contrast, the contact characteristics were dramatically modified to exhibit the behavior of an Ohmic contact by sweeping the applied bias from -0.8 V to 0.8 V in both cases of Al/ETL/Si structures with distinct NCQD concentrations of NCQD-PEI ETL (0.05 % and 0.10 %). This change can be attributed to improved conductance, which attests to the augmentation of electron transfer through the contact. However, a non-linear I-V curve was displayed when NCQD concentration increased to 0.15 %, which can be ascribed to relatively uneven surfaces due to NCQD aggregation that causes uncontrolled contacts under the condition of sophisticated interfaces.

Surface potential examinations stand for the keen interpretation of band features and interfacial charge transfer in the vicinity of heterostructure contact, which are performed on the Si substrates with two types of ETL coating via KPFM analysis, as demonstrated in Fig. 4(b) and (c). The results manifest that the PEI and NCQD-PEI sides display the surface potential differences (SPDs) of 368.21 mV and 444.70 mV compared with that of n-Si substrates, respectively, which drives the charge transfer from n-Si to both types of ETLs due to relatively negative surface potential at ETL sides [39]. Moreover, NCQD-PEI as the ETL design possessing the larger SPD compared with that of sole PEI is clearly visualized, where the work function of n-Si with ETL coating is estimated to be 4.40 eV (Si modified with PEI) and 4.33 eV (Si modified with NCQDs-PEI), respectively, according to Eq. 1,

where SPD the surface potential difference between a tungsten tip and the tested film, e the electron charge and work functions of tip and sample are denoted by WF tip and WFfilm, respectively. In addition, by depositing Al layer (150 nm) on ETL/Si substrates, the correlated SPD values between bare Al and Al modified with ETL were examined, as depicted in Fig. 4(d) and (e). The results reveal that the SPDs of 435.79 mV (Al modified with PEI) and 504.67 mV (Al modified with NCQD-PEI) with respect to bare Al, respectively, attesting that the transfer of electrons via interfaces modified with NCQD-PEI is strongly facilitated driven from the larger SPD, where the extracted SPD values are summarized in Table 1 . Consequently, the direction of electrons is clearly directed from n-Si to Al through the NCQD-PEI based ETL. These results support the fact that PEI and NCQD-PEI serve as the efficient electron transport materials that facilitate the electron collection by the Al cathode. Moreover, the PEI matrix incorporated with NCQDs with the adjustment of filler concentration seems to be promising for raising the SPD across the modified contact between Al electrode and n-Si substrate, which will be discussed later.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Illustration of band energy diagram of Al/ETL film/n-Si upon the effect of interfacial dipole evolution
ce:section.ce:para: Interfaces between organic materials and metal surfaces have been extensively studied [40], while the correlated organic/metal interaction still requires more examinations. Plausible effects that may be taken into consideration for interpreting the microscopic interactions when organic materials are in contact with metals can be categorized into three major aspects: First, the polarization of electron density at metal surfaces due to molecule adsorption is endowed. Next, the partial charge transfer is involved through covalent organic-metal bonds and finally, the integer charge transfer through tunnelling effect across the organic/metal interfaces [41]. In terms of the involved interaction strength, the microscopic physics at interfaces can be further classified by physisorption and chemisorption. Physisorption, stemming from the variation of metal work function due to the presence or absence of an organic adsorbate, which is normally envisioned as relatively weak molecule/metal interactions. On the other hand, the chemisorption effect, owing from the coupled effect between π-conjugated molecules with metal surfaces that causes the partial charge transfer emerged as relatively strong molecule/metal interactions [40,41]. In general, the boundary set between physisorption, and chemisorption lies in the correspondence of induced dipoles associated with the creation of interfaces.

The presence of interface dipole takes part in the modulation on electronic levels and work function of metal [42,43]. The electron injection barrier (ΔEbarrier, e −) is suppressed under the consequence of metal work function decrease due to the involvement of the interface dipole via organic-mediated dipole induced effect, as depicted in Fig. 5(a). For the reverse condition, the increase in metal work function couples out the raising value of ΔEbarrier, e −, which inhibits the electron injection toward organic layer.

The outcome of coupling of strong interface dipole due to chemisorption effect can also lead to the modulation of electronic configuration of metal surface, which is mainly caused by the partial charge transfer. The microscopic origin can be understood by the rearrangement of electron densities due to orbital overlapping at interfaces, where the partial charge transfer is found between the adsorbate and metal site. The resultant chemical potential (µ), expressed by, µ=
(
d E
d N
)
ν ext
, is visualized to describe the direction of charge transfer when two materials with distinct electron densities are brought into contact that allow the spatial charge rearrangement, where E the electronic energy, N the number of electrons at constant external potential (
ν ext
(r)) are defined [43]. To equalize their electronic chemical potentials for reaching the dynamic equilibrium, the net charge transfer created by the chemisorption effect induces the spectral shift (up-shift or down-shift) of the vacuum level, which consequently modulates the metal work function and direction of charge transfer.

Experimental band feature examinations of PEI and NCQD/PEI ETLs were conducted by UPS analysis [Fig. 5(b)], where the extracted parameters are summarized in Table 2 . By considering the estimated values of secondary electron cutoff (SEC) and valence band maximum (VBM) obtained from UPS spectra and bandgap energy of ETLs measured from UV/Vis spectra [Fig. 1(a)]. The energies of High Occupied Molecular Orbital (HOMO), Lowest Unoccupied Molecular Orbital (LUMO) and work function can be expressed as follows [46,47],

where hν the incident photon energy (21.2 eV for He I). The work functions of pristine Al and Al layers modified with two types ETLs are extracted from Eqs. (2)-(4), presenting the reduction of Al work function toward 3.45 eV and 3.23 eV under ETL insertion using PEI and NCQD-PEI, respectively [Fig. 5(b)], compared to pristine work function of Al (4.3 eV). The results explicitly point out the effective reduction of the Al work function by employing NCQD-PEI as ETL that function as feasible route to modulate the electronic state at Al surfaces and thus facilitate the interfacial charge transfer.

To get insights into the band-structure modification, the band energy diagrams of the created Si/Al interfaces after reaching the equilibrium are illustrated by considering the involvements of PEI [Fig. 5(c)] and NCQD-PEI [Fig. 5(d)] ETLs between Al and n-Si, respectively. In the case of PEI-based ETL, the band bending of Al electrode displaying the chemical potential difference (Δ) of 0.85 eV is achieved in contact with n-Si that asserts the argument the electron transfer from n-Si to Al electrode. Remarkably, the value of Δ increases to 1.17 eV when involving the NCQD-PEI as the ETL design, and these features are speculated by the significant evolution of interface dipole that modifies the electronic state at Al surfaces and remedies the work-function modulation. The microscopic origin regarding the molecule/metal interactions is further undertaken by the interface dipole evolution that visualizes the negative and positive poles at Al/Si interfaces. Specifically, the negative pole is directing to the Al electrode by the electron pair donors from ETL interfacial structures, hence leaving the positive pole directing to organic layer. This phenomenon causes the downshifted vacuum level energy (EVAC), resulting in the reduction of Al work function determined by the extent of involved Δ, as evidenced in experimental UPS characterizations [Fig. 5(b)].

The origins of interface dipole evolution after ETL contact are stemmed from the physisorption and chemisorption [43–46]. It remains puzzled to decouple the origins of interface dipoles due to physical or chemical adsorption route as the present features are contributed from the strong coupling between these two effects. Nevertheless, the more pronounced reduction of Al work function in the presence of NCQD-PEI ETL underlines the overall effects of push back (physisorption) and charge transfer (chemisorption) become more significant in comparison to that of bare PEI ETL. It has been reported that the electron density tail of Al is shortened by the physiosorbed organic PEI molecules via the push-back effect, which causes the lowered vacuum level and results in the reduction of the Al work function [48]. The addition of NCQDs within PEI matrix as hybrid ETL can further repulse the surface free electrons and strengthen the push-back effect in physisorption concept due to the presence of electron-donor groups including hydroxyl group, amine, pyridine and pyrrolidine at out-shells of NCQDs, which can be experimentally interpreted by the presence of larger surface potential difference compared to bare PEI ETLs on Al metal, as evidenced in Fig. 4.

On the other hand, the hybrid features of incorporating NCQDs within the PEI matrix modify the electronic configurations of PEI chains, causing charge rearrangement due to orbital overlapping. Meanwhile, the high carrier concentration of NCQD fillers, measured from Mott-Schottky analysis [Fig. 6(a)], can effectively provide the enriched electron-donor sites within the hybrid ETLs, which facilitate the chemisorption effect via partial charge transfer [46]. The implications can be further envisioned by XPS spectra, as shown in Fig. 6(b). It was found that the central Al 2p signal of bare Al deposited on Si substrate is located at 73.9 eV, which is assigned to the Al-O bonding contributed from formation of native oxide at Al surfaces [49]. The chemical features of Al 2p spectra are varied under the cases where the thin Al layer is deposited on PEI/Si and NCQD-PEI/Si, as presented in the middle and figure below of Fig. 6(b), respectively. The slight red shift of Al-O signature (approximately 0.1 eV) is visualized from thin Al/PEI/Si, and explicit red shift (approximately 0.3 eV) is displayed from thin Al/NCQD-PEI/Si. It implicates that the extent of electron donation reaches the highest when sandwiching the Al/Si contact with NCQD-PEI structures. Thus, the hybrid NCQD-PEI as the protype of ETL for Al/Si contact can couple out the results of further reduction in Al work function and supply the efficient electron transport and collection.

The employment of developed NCQD-PEI ETLs was readily tested in the construction of Si-based organic/inorganic hybrid solar cells and the resulting photovoltaic performances were compared with various types of ETL designs [50–53]. Recently, the employments of passivated tin oxide [54] and azahomofullerene/tin oxide [55] have been reported to serve as the efficient electron transporting materials for realizing high-performance perovskite solar cells due to improved charge extraction and transfer capabilities. In this work, the cell structures essentially consist of Ag grids as the top electrode, PEDOT:PSS layer as the hole transport layer and p-type component, n-type SiNW/Si substrates as the n-type component, and finally the Al layer modified with ETL structures as the rear electrode, as illustrated in Fig. 7(a). The measured photovoltaic current density-voltage (J-V) results are displayed in Fig. 7(b), and the related cell parameters are summarized in Table 3 . It can be found that the foremost power conversion efficiency (PCE) of tested solar cells is obtained using NCQD-PEI (0.10 % NCQDs) as the ETL, achieving a PCE of 13.59 %, J sc of 37.66 mA/cm2, V oc of 525 mV and a fill factor of 68.76 %. Meanwhile, a relatively low series resistance (R s) of 0.52 Ohms as well as high shunt resistance (R sh) of 205.3 Ohms were also achieved, in comparison to reference cells (R s= 1.7 Ohms and R sh= 51.0 Ohm), cells with ETL (0.05 % NCQDs) (R s= 1.3 Ohms and R sh= 203.8 Ohm) and cells with ETL (0.15 % NCQDs) (R s= 1.83 Ohms and R sh= 312.64 Ohm), respectively.

The features of improved Rs and Rsh explicitly point out that an efficient ETL design can indeed reduce the interfacial resistances on photocurrent collection and in turn alleviate the power loss of solar cells [56]. In addition, the spectral results of external quantum efficiency (EQE) and long-term examination of fabricated solar cells are discussed in Figs. S3 of Supplementary material, illustrating the efficient light harvesting and photon-to-charge conversion with long-term stability of ETL-employed hybrid solar cells [57]. To gain further insight into the contact behaviors of Si/Ai in the constructed solar cells, the ideality factor (n), reverse saturation current density (J s) and Schottky barrier height (ϕB) of solar cells are examined, as presented in Fig. 7(c) and summarized in Table 4 . In addition, the non-unity of ideality factor is considered indicative of electron-hole recombination existed in organic solar cells [35,36]. It was found that the n reached the lowest value (1.86) when the NCQD-PEI (0.10 %) interlayer was inserted, which represents the effective suppression of charge recombination of devices among the tested solar cells, in accordance with the EQE results [Fig. S3(a) of Supplementary material]. Furthermore, the value of J s substantially decreased to 2.75×10−6A compared to that of reference cells (J s= 4.35×10−5A). According to the relation between J s and V oc [Section S3 of the Supplementary material], the decreased J s can reasonably explain the enhanced V oc of devices with improved rear contacts. Besides, the maximum ϕB reaches 0.804 eV under the condition of NCQD-PEI (0.10 %) of ETL constitution, illustrating the improved rectifying characteristics resulting from the overall interfacial contacts. Since the only modified contact in various solar cells is in the Al/Si contact as all the fabrication parameters and experimental conditions are strictly controlled, we can septulate that improved electron transport and collection at Si/NCQD-PEI/Al interfaces that contributes to the reduction of power loss in cell devices. The impacts of various ETL designs on efficiency enhancement of hybrid solar cells are presented in Table S1, evidencing the effectiveness of the current NCQD-PEI ETL that readily benefits the improvement of cell performances [3,58–62]. Finally, the proposed band diagrams of the interface between Al and n-SiNWs without and with ETL interlayer are schematically presented in Fig. 7(d) and (e), respectively. Note that all the quantities displayed in the band diagrams were experimentally determined. Compared with typical Schottky contact of Al/n-SiNWs without the involvement of ETL interlayer, the downward band bending in the vicinity of the metal (Al)/semiconductor (n-SiNW/Si substrates) contact with the involvement of NCQD-PEI based ETL is encountered due to the evolution of interface dipoles that dramatically modify the interfacial charge-transfer characteristics via the reduction of Al work function. The present method for processing the ETL structures is industry-compatible due to the involvement of inexpensive materials, low-synthetic temperature and spin-coating process, which can be directly integrated into standard manufacturing procedures of crystalline Si solar cells [63,64]. Further improvement in large-area uniformity and reliability is required for the practical applications, and the capability of the current ETL designs for non-Si substrates requires further investigation.

In conclusion, the low-cost, reliable and rapid microwave synthesis of NCQDs as fillers within the PEI matrix is found to be the promising ETL design for the modification of the Al/Si contact. By employing detailed materials and electric characterizations, we find that the mechanism of interface dipole evolution can be explicated for the modulation of the Al work function and results in the consequence of augment of electron-transfer and collection characteristics. In addition, the band structures and estimation of work-function variations were examined by KPFM and UPS investigations, illustrating the explicit reduction of the Al work function to 3.23 eV based on the optimized constitutional design (0.10 % NCQD in PEI), which can be interpreted by the overall outcome of physisorption and chemisorption effects. The results show the great feasibility and capability of work-function modulation at Al/Si contact compared with conventional polymer-based surface treatment. Finally, the practical employment of designed NCQD-PEI hybrid ETL on Si-based hybrid solar cells is found to be efficient for the improvement of cell efficiency over 30.9 % and compared with reference cells without PEI utilization. The enhancement of conversion efficiency presented in this work is found to be superior to other reported ETL materials for the design of organic/inorganic hybrid solar cells, paving the sound practicality for various photovoltaic, optoelectronic and photonic applications.
